era_context: Early Classical Greece; Darius I's Persian Empire consolidating western territories; Confucius in his mid-40s, active in Lu; Iron Age Europe; the Mauryan Empire not yet established in India.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of the Indian subcontinent (sparse written records)

events.title: Cleisthenes' democratic reforms in Athens (traditional date range)
events.description: Cleisthenes, often called the 'father of Athenian democracy,' implemented sweeping constitutional reforms that reorganized the Athenian state. These reforms included the creation of ten new tribes (phylai) based on geography rather than kinship, the establishment of the Council of 500 (Boule), and the institution of ostracism. The reforms were designed to break the power of aristocratic families and distribute political power more broadly among male citizens. The traditional date for these reforms is 508/507 BCE, though some scholars place them slightly later. By 506 BCE, these reforms were likely in early implementation.

sources.name: Aristotle, Constitution of the Athenians, written ~330 BCE

events.certainty_note: The reforms are well-attested in later sources, but the exact chronology is debated. Most scholars place them in 508/507 BCE, making 506 BCE part of the implementation phase. The dating uncertainty is ±2–3 years.

events.title: Spartan intervention in Athens (Isagoras and Cleomenes I)
events.description: Following Cleisthenes' reforms, the Spartan king Cleomenes I intervened in Athenian politics, supporting the aristocrat Isagoras against Cleisthenes. Cleomenes invaded Attica and attempted to overturn the democratic reforms. However, the Athenian people rose in support of Cleisthenes, and Cleomenes was forced to withdraw. This episode is traditionally dated to 506 BCE and represents an early conflict between oligarchic and democratic forces in Greece. The Spartan intervention ultimately failed, and Cleisthenes' reforms were consolidated.

sources.name: Aristotle, Constitution of the Athenians, written ~330 BCE

events.certainty_note: The event is well-attested in ancient sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Herodotus and Aristotle place it in the context of Cleisthenes' reforms, traditionally dated to 508/507 BCE. The dating to 506 BCE is conventional but could be off by 1–2 years.

events.title: Confucius travels and teaching in China
events.region: State of Lu and surrounding regions, China
events.description: Confucius, now in his mid-40s, was actively engaged in teaching and advising rulers in the State of Lu and possibly traveling to neighboring states. This period marks the height of his influence as a moral and political philosopher. He was developing the core teachings that would later be compiled in the Analects (Lunyu), emphasizing ritual propriety (li), filial piety (xiao), and virtuous governance. His influence on Chinese thought and governance would be profound, though recognition of his importance came largely after his death.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled ~109 BCE
Lunyu (Analects), compiled by disciples, date uncertain

events.certainty_note: Confucius's life dates (551–479 BCE) are traditionally accepted, and his active teaching period is placed in his 40s–60s. However, no contemporary documentation exists. The Analects and Shiji were compiled centuries later and may contain legendary or misplaced material.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Cleisthenes' reforms to 508/507 BCE is debated by modern scholars; some place them slightly later. The Spartan intervention under Cleomenes I is attested in ancient sources but the exact date is uncertain. Confucius's activities in 506 BCE are inferred from traditional chronologies but lack contemporary documentation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 506 BCE is dominated by Greek sources (Herodotus, Aristotle), both written well after the events. These sources are subject to chronological confusion, anecdotal bias, and possible legendary embellishment. Chinese sources (Shiji, Analects) are similarly late compilations. Archaeological evidence (pottery, inscriptions) is sparse for this specific year. The record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events in the Mediterranean and China; other regions and social strata are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Cleisthenes' reforms and the establishment of ostracism are part of the same democratic transformation
Spartan intervention was a reaction to Cleisthenes' democratic reforms
Confucius's teaching career spans from his 40s to his death in 479 BCE
